responseSet.title: Letter to the Editor
responseSet.authorMast: Lauri R. Robertson and  Michael Eigen
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letter to the Editor

Lauri Robertson Ph.D., M.D. and Michael Eigen PHD

Michael Eigen

113 Canner Street
New Haven, CT 06511

225 Central Park West (Apt. 101A)
New York, NY 10024

Michael Eigen  

To the Editor:

It was an unexpected pleasure to receive the inaugural issue of Psychoanalytic Dialogues. As a psychiatric resident worried that psychoanalysis is in danger of becoming an historic relic of interest only to literary critics, I read it straight through, murmuring affirmatives. Dr. Mitchell's editorial philosophy (“respectful yet playful”) seems pitched exactly where the real dialogue between clinicians is. Healing fractures in the “sustained and dedicated effort to discover and articulate personal meanings” is crucial to seeing psychoanalysis and perhaps all psychodynamic treatment safely into the future.

I was alarmed, however, by Dr. Eigen's concluding paper “Boa and Flowers.” While appreciative of his vivid portrait of “Janice” and his impassioned frankness, I know this patient all too well. Janice is the borderline who gives our “neutral” diagnostic term its pejorative connotation. What therapist isn't unsettled by a patient who, with a trail of discarded treaters behind, makes an instant, plaintive, hostile demand to, in effect, “Love me and fix me?” Eigen says, “How tantalized I was with her sense of rightness….” This is precisely what alarmed me.

Irrespective of the countertransference (in the broad sense) elicited by the baldness of Janice's articulate appeal — guilt, inadequacy, rescue fantasies, fury, pity, annoyance, love — to imagine that she is right, that she knows what she needs from a therapist, is a serious failure of empathy that makes the therapist hostage to the very monster (the “boa”) that is strangling her. To accept Janice at her word amounts to “siding with the good side of the split,” which will inevitably result in dissolution of the treatment in the abyss of her need, rage, envy and terror of boundary loss. In is neither cold nor uncaring to say “no” to Janice; in the long run, it is only helpful.

But, as Eigen makes painfully clear, Janice cannot be engaged by “no,” or even his furtive “yes, but….” Perhaps she will make her way without treatment or, as her former therapist believes, she needs a hospital.

responseSet.title: Commentary on “Deconstructing Difference: Gender, Splitting, and Transitional Space”
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Commentary on “Deconstructing Difference: Gender, Splitting, and Transitional Space”

Juliet Mitchell

Juliet Mitchell

275 Lonsdale
London SW13
England

Juliet Mitchell  

I am going to use Muriel Dimen's most interesting article to express some general concerns I have had for a long time about feminist concepts of gender within the use of psychoanalytic theory. It is somewhat unfair to treat her work thus symptomatically, but I hope my “reply” will be regarded as it is intended, as the opening of a friendly debate between co-workers in the field.

The important and interesting ideas in Dimen's article are, for me, those in which she extends Winnicott's notion of transitional space and of paradox to reconsider the relationship of dualisms, particularly of femininity and masculinity. Dimen's loose use of the concept of splitting to cover cultural dichotomies indifferently with an intrapsychic process seems to create more problems than it solves. My greatest difficulty is with her particular feminist use of the term “gender” in the context of psychoanalytic theory.

For Winnicott paradox means the holding together of two apparently opposed perspectives. His concept of transitional space is of the area where this possibility comes into being. In this space both mother and infant are engaged in separating themselves from a primary fusion; here they can move in and out of feeling themselves to be the same person and experiencing their difference from each other. It is the area where one creates the object that is already there, a lifelong place of play and rest in which there is no necessary distinction between inner and outer, subject and object.

Dimen agrees with Winnicott that the psychoanalytic session can offer the possibility of paradox. Indeed, I think it can and should.

responseSet.title: Commentary on “On Intimacy Between Men”
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Commentary on “On Intimacy Between Men”

Irene Fast PHD

Irene Fast

580 Union Drive
Ann Arbor, MI 48109

Irene Fast  

From the time he first enunciated it, Freud's conception of women's gender development has been subject to vigorous criticism. No comparable reexamination has been addressed to his conception of gender development in men. Yet, since masculinity and femininity are regularly found to be coordinate developments, it is likely that if Freud's conceptions of women's development are seriously flawed, his views of gender development in men have coordinate difficulties. Here Kaftal's paper makes a significant contribution.

Kaftal suggests that Freud's work, taken as a whole, is a meditation on what it is to be a man. He argues that Freud's images of manhood are consistently military and heroic, embodied in such figures as Kronos, Oedipus, Narcissus, Faust, and Moses. They are images of the brave, isolated human mind, dependent on reason alone for guidance through the thickets of biological irrationality and rapacity. A man's life story, in Freud's view, turns on decisive individual confrontation with the other in which manhood and death are inextricably bound together.

Kaftal, however, argues that although Freud was very clear about the dynamics that contribute to this heroic subtext, he was unable satisfactorily to elaborate the dynamics of men's tender relations to one another. He did speak of the boy's early special relation to his father in which he takes the father as his ideal. But that relationship did not enter into his conception of the oedipal crisis or of the identifications with the father and the consolidation of the superego in which it is resolved. Individuality remained central. Health was equated with the primacy of reason and the mastery of emotion. Group thought, or interchange among men, was more emotional and so more primitive, a residue of men's animal heritage.

Kaftal's contribution brings men's relational and affective development to the fore.

responseSet.title: Reply to Mitchell
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reply to Mitchell

Muriel Dimen PHD

Muriel Dimen

Muriel Dimen  

Repetition? I think not. Or, at least, it's not I who repeat, but gender. Let me explain.

What Mitchell's Freud deemed conclusive is not final; whether sexuality necessarily seals the distinction between self and other is an open question, not a quasi-fact. As far as I can tell, Mitchell construes sexual difference thus because of its procreative function, not its unconscious or cultural meaning. Her argument—that sexuality's ultimate purpose is to facilitate the conjugation of two engendered beings so that they may “engender”—not only effaces desire, it also reprises the least creative, dialectical, and psychoanalytic part of Freud, that is, his scientific wish to ground psychology in biology, a project that Mitchell elsewhere disavows.

I do not believe that repeated appeal to Freud (one of Mitchell's rhetorical strategies) adjudicates any given argument. However, I want to point out that my project of understanding sexual difference as in tension with selfhood, not as the guardian either of the psyche or of the species, is one that Freud himself also inaugurated but could not carry out because he lacked the notion of “gender” as a simultaneously psychological and cultural category. With sexuality reframed by gender, however, a feminist-informed psychoanalysis is positioned to complete that very project. Instead, then, of arguing that our ancestors said it all before us, I suggest we steal the Promethean fire and forge ahead. That you were once a gleam in someone's eye doesn't mean there can't be anything new under the sun.

Pun in hand, let us return to gender. Mitchell's reading of Freud proposes a developmental line in which (sexual) difference emerges from sameness.

© 1991 The Analytic Press

359

responseSet.title: Boa and Flowers
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Boa and Flowers

Michael Eigen PHD

Michael Eigen

225 Central Park West, #101A
New York, NY 10024

Michael Eigen  

This article presents the author's reactions to a client who saw him for one visit, came 20 minutes late, demanded ideal “parenting,” called during the following two weeks, and one day left flowers in his waiting room. The focus is on the failed meeting, the meeting that did not take place, loss, and longing. The aim is to go beyond diagnostic categories to the place where therapy fails, the edge of what is therapeutically possible.

Dwelling with therapeutic incapacity in a full and detailed way—letting “it” speak—may help to stimulate evolution of capacities needed to work with individuals who now slip away from what therapy can do.

responseSet.title: Reply to Discussion by Enid Balint
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reply to Discussion by Enid Balint

Philip Bromberg PHD

Philip Bromberg

300 Central Park West
New York, N.Y. 10024

Philip Bromberg  

Participating with Mrs. Balint in this dialogue is a special pleasure for me, and I would like to thank her for the great care with which she read my paper and for directly responding to its central points not only in their immediate context but in their broader implications as well. It is no small accomplishment that she was able to discuss with such clarity and depth the ideas of someone representing an alternative school of thought and also to sacrifice neither the areas of interface with her own viewpoint nor the shades of contrast and differences in emphasis. I am particularly appreciative of the graceful style—one might say, the aesthetics of her writing—with which she expresses those thoughts that touch on issues of possible disagreement. It is a style that is understated and gentle in tone—reflective rather than rhetorical, but nonetheless penetrating and creatively evocative. Aesthetics and its relevance to the psychoanalytic process are, of course, the issue that the present paper has attempted to address, at least in one of its aspects, and to which I would like to return somewhat more broadly in my reply to Mrs. Balint's discussion. Consider the following statement by Levenson (1972):

[T]he core of psychotherapy lies in the delineation of pattern, the formal structure of events that constitutes what might be called the aesthetics of experience—aesthetics not in the vulgate sense of beauty and light but, more precisely, meaning sensitivity and perception, that is, concerned with the forms and organization of sensory experience, rather than content and meaning [p. 211].

Nowhere is this emphasis better represented than by Enid Balint's portrayal of the analytic relationship as facilitating the patient's ability to experience himself as an “I” that can freely assert, “This is not me” as well as “This is me.” Her vision of the patient's “self” as an entity that is owned by the patient rather than the core of the patient is not simply a matter of semantic preference or conceptual hairsplitting.

responseSet.title: The Silent Patient
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Silent Patient

Nina Coltart MD

Nina Coltart

1A, Well Road
Hampstead
London NW3 1LJ
England

Nina Coltart  

The silent patient is defined as one who speaks for less than 10% of the entire analytic treatment period, and for many sessions, not at all. This article derives from the author's experience in the analyses of eight such patients during her working life as an analyst (30 years). After a brief discussion of some basic theoretical issues that are usually kept in mind in the early stages of such analyses, there follows an examination of the particular technical problems presented by extended silence; short clinical vignettes, illustrative of these problems, are woven into this part of the text. Special emphasis attaches to the detailed study and use of the countertransference, probably more valuable in these cases than in any others. The qualities that are desirable, even necessary, in the analyst are discussed, among them the capacity for love, a subject still approached gingerly in our literature. Reference is made to two eccentric forms of silence: absence and sleep. Notes on the study of body language and the uses of humor are discussed briefly. There is a special interest inherent in the work with silent patients, and some thoughts on its rather mysterious quality close the article.

responseSet.title: On Knowing One's Patient Inside Out: The Aesthetics of Unconscious Communication
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Knowing One's Patient Inside Out: The Aesthetics of Unconscious Communication**

Philip Bromberg PHD

Philip Bromberg

300 Central Park West
New York, N.Y. 10024

Philip Bromberg  

“Knowing one's patient inside out” is a metaphor that is intended to capture the paradoxical quality of the intersubjective field that we call the analytic relationship. The interface among trauma, dissociation, and regression is discussed in the context of unconscious communication as a transferential enactment of unsymbolized experience. The view is offered that for certain patients in particular, past experience is not so much unconscious as “frozen in time” and that a key element of the psychoanalytic relationship is bridging dissociated aspects of self through the creation of a dyadic experiential field that is both “inside” and “outside.” The writings of Michael Balint, D. W. Winnicott, and several other British object relational theorists are explored in the context of a contemporary interpersonal psychoanalytic perspective.

responseSet.title: Discussion: Toward a Social-Constructivist View of the Psychoanalytic Situation
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Discussion: Toward a Social-Constructivist View of the Psychoanalytic Situation

Irwin Hoffman PHD

Irwin Hoffman

55 E. Washington Street, Suite 1217
Chicago, IL 60602

Irwin Hoffman  

The three papers by Modell, Aron, and Greenberg are discussed in terms of their relationship to a new paradigm for understanding the psychoanalytic situation. The paradigm is called social-constructivist to capture both the idea of the analyst's participation and the idea of construction of meaning. It is argued that these theorists, as well as many of the authors they cite as part of a broad movement in the field, do not consistently meet the criteria for this paradigm, although they seem to be aiming for it. An important source of inconsistency and confusion derives from the confounding of the two axes: drive-relational and positivist-constructivist. Many relational theorists who hold fast to the idea that analysts can grasp the truth of both their own experience and that of the patient are no closer to the constructivist point of view than was Freud. The call by Aron and Greenberg for greater attention to the patient's resisted experience of the analyst's subjectivity is discussed in terms of its potential benefits and problems. The ritualized asymmetry of the psychoanalytic situation is said to have important functions, including prevention of excessive involvement and protection of the unobjectionable positive transference and of a degree of idealization. Modell's notion of paradox, which makes the therapeutic relationship seem “real” and “unreal” at the same time, is seen as a special instance of the always precarious social construction of reality. It is argued, moreover, that the social and individual aspects of experience are interdependent. Neither is reducible to the other, and both should be understood, like many other issues in the new paradigm, in terms of a dialectical interplay of figure and ground in experience.

Parts of this discussion, particularly those addressed to Lewis Aron's paper, were presented previously at the meetings of the Division of Psychoanalysis of the American Psychological Association, New York City, April 5, 1990.

Irwin Z. Hoffman is Associate Professor of Clinical Psychiatry and Behavioral Sciences, Institute of Psychiatry, Northwestern University Medical School.

responseSet.title: Gender as Contradiction
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Gender as Contradiction

Adrienne Harris PHD

Adrienne Harris

80 University Place, 5th Fl.
New York, 10009

Adrienne Harris  

A model of gender as a paradoxical and multidimensional structure is proposed. An extended critical reading of Freud's essay on a case of homosexuality in a woman undermines the notion of gender identity and sexual object choice as monolithic categories of experience. The Freud case is considered for its radical model of sexuality and gender, but also for its restrictive use of classical interpretation. Alternative interpretive lines and transference countertransference meanings are considered. With the use of contemporary clinical material, a model of gender identity and sexuality is proposed in which the unconscious and symbolic meaning of bodies and genders, rather than biological sex of the lover and the beloved, carries the interpretive weight.

responseSet.title: Father and Daughter: Identification with Difference — A Contribution to Gender Heterodoxy
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Father and Daughter: Identification with Difference — A Contribution to Gender Heterodoxy

Jessica Benjamin PHD

Jessica Benjamin

228 West 22nd St.
New York, NY 10011

Jessica Benjamin  

This paper reinterprets the historical problem of penis envy, that is, the girl's wish to be masculine, in terms of a developmental need to identify with father. Many of the problems posed by earlier analyses of femininity can be clarified by recognizing that before the girl “truns” to the oedipal father as love object, she looks to the rapprochement father for identification. Identification is not merely an internal structure, it is a relationship in which the subject recognizes herself or himself in the other. In rapprochement, love of the father, who symbolically represents the outside world, takes the place of the practicing toddler's “love affair with the world.” This identificatory love of the father, initially noticed in boys by Freud and later authors, is often frustrated by the father's absence or inability to recognize the daughter. This frustrated longing takes the penis as its symbol of likeness. The pre-oedipal over-inclusive phase of identification with the other sex parent is not superceded by the oedipal constellation, but is integrated with it. Thus identification becomes an important basis of the love of the other: it is not so much the opposite of object love as an important precursor and ongoing constituent of it. Case material illustrates the multiple possibilities of identificatory love and use of phallic symbolism to represent them.

responseSet.title: Symposium on Gender: Introduction
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Symposium on Gender: Introduction

Adrienne Harris PHD

Adrienne Harris

Adrienne Harris  

The papers and commentaries in this symposium constitute a new line of development in the debates about gender that have been evolving in and around psychoanalysis. Freud's famous remark “What does woman want?” initiates what is by now a long and often heated conversation. To introduce these papers and commentaries on the problems of gender, I want to provide a brief review of their historical context, to give a narrative of the conversation thus far. The articles in this symposium are informed by feminism, by the studies of infancy and early development, by postmodern debates on the nature of subjectivity, and by relational models within psychoanalysis. Presenting this symposium in the inaugural year of this journal seems timely and appropriate because the questions raised here—the paradox of gender as fixed and fluid, the boundaries of biology, culture, and psyche, the interdependence of intrapsychic and social, the meaning of gender in relationships—address, in the realm of gender studies, the central concerns and philosophy of Psychoanalytic Dialogues.

To begin, I am going to conjure up one of the most unsettling of Freud's images of the relations of men and women. Offering a psychoanalytic reading of the mythological tale of the warrior who freezes in fear at the sight of the head of Medusa, Freud proposes that the “terror of Medusa is the terror of castration” (Freud, 1953). This image of horror, a woman's head ringed with snakes, Freud promotes as a mythical metaphor of the terrible sight of the female genital. “The sight of Medusa's head makes the spectator stiff with terror, turns him to stone.” This stiffness signifies an erection and thus a confirmation that “he is still in possession of a penis and the stiffening reassures him of the fact.” I will add one narrative detail, missing from Freud's reading. The Medusa is killed by Perseus, who defeats her dread power by gazing at her image reflected in a mirror.

responseSet.title: Editorial Philosophy
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial Philosophy

Stephen Mitchell PHD

Stephen Mitchell

Stephen Mitchell  

There is a great Irony at the heart of contemporary psychoanalysis. The skilled psychoanalyst as clinician is, perhaps, the most careful and systematic listener, the most precise and respectful speaker, the most highly trained and refined communicator, that Western culture has produced. A sustained and dedicated effort to discover and articulate the personal meanings, the inner logic of the patient's communications, is the most fundamental dimension of the craft of psychoanalysis in all its variations. Yet, psychoanalysts have enormous difficulty listening and speaking meaningfully to each other.

Psychoanalysts cluster in groups identified with a particular tradition, each with its own favorite words and phrases, each usually founded by a single charismatic leader. When analysts within one group speak with or read each other's work, there is a sense that they stand on familiar ground. The shared language, the key words (“instinctual drive,” “structural model,” “interpersonal reality,” “selfobject function,” “internal object,” “transitional object”) have meaning far greater than that of the referents of the terms themselves. The language creates a sense of familiarity, an experience of belonging to a community that shares a common sensibility, a consensually accepted vision of the complex and subtle workings of the psyche, a road map and repertoire of techniques for negotiating the storms and ambiguities of the psychoanalytic process.

To the uninitiated, to outsiders (vis-à-vis any particular psychoanalytic subculture), the same key words and phrases do not evoke a sense of familiarity and shared beliefs but, on the contrary, are often grating and off-putting. Each phrase carries with it many assumptions with which one may not agree. One has the feeling that the speakers of these phrases just do not understand what is important. The language of another perspective alienates and makes one uneasy and generates a longing for the familiar, more secure, and hospitable strains of one's own theoretical discourse.

responseSet.title: What Is a Selfobject?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

What Is a Selfobject?

Joseph Lichtenberg MD

Joseph Lichtenberg

Joseph Lichtenberg  

This paper evaluates Kohut's selfobject concept by reviewing the historical context of its origin and the gradual substitution of an emphasis on experience for the ego psychological emphasis on function. When viewed as a dimension of experience, a selfobject experience has the affective quality of vitalization. This modification and extension of Kohut's original concept derives from infant research, especially Stern's revision of affect theory and Lichtenberg's concept of five motivational systems. The paper concludes with an application of the theory of selfobject experience to pathological states, especially those involving addiction.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Patient's Experience of the Analyst's Subjectivity

Lewis Aron PHD

Lewis Aron

243 West End Avenue, Apt. 310
New York, N.Y. 10023

Lewis Aron  

This article highlights the analysis of the patient's experience of the analyst's subjectivity in the psychoanalytic situation. Just as psychoanalytic theory has focused on the mother exclusively as the object of the infant's needs while ignoring the subjectivity of the mother, so, too, psychoanalysis has considered the analyst only as an object while neglecting the subjectivity of the analyst as the analyst is experienced by the patient. The analyst's subjectivity is an important element in the analytic situation, and the patient's experience of the analyst's subjectivity needs to be made conscious.

Patients seek to connect to their analysts, to know them, to probe beneath their professional facade, and to reach their psychic centers much in the same way that children seek to connect to and penetrate their parents' inner worlds. The exploration of the patient's experience of the analyst's subjectivity represents one underemphasized aspect of the analysis of transference, and it is an essential aspect of a detailed and thorough explication and articulation of the therapeutic relationship. The paper explores controversies regarding the analyst's self-disclosure and countertransference.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Contemporary Perspectives on Self: Toward an Integration

Stephen Mitchell PHD

Stephen Mitchell

251 W. 71st Street
New York, NY 10023

Stephen Mitchell  

Formulations concerning self have been a predominant concern of psychoanalytic theorizing over the past several decades; many different definitions have been employed, and many different perspectives explored. Despite the diversity of definitions and multiplicity of approaches, certain consistent themes and concerns have characterized postclassical theorizing and mark their difference from classical psychoanalysis. This article explores the view of the self as multiplex and discontinuous, as developed in both object relations theories and in interpersonal psychoanalysis. This approach to self is contrasted and interfaced with the view of the self as continuous and integral, as developed within self psychology.

responseSet.title: Deconstructing Difference: Gender, Splitting, and Transitional Space
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Deconstructing Difference: Gender, Splitting, and Transitional Space

Muriel Dimen PHD

Muriel Dimen

312 W. 20th Street
New York, NY 10011

Muriel Dimen  

Apparently a straightforward elaboration of anatomical difference, “gender” is symbolically tied to many kinds of cultural representations, which, in turn, set the terms not only for understanding the relations between women and men but for organizing self-experience. Consequently, problems of self may come to be coded in terms of gender, and those of gender, in terms of the self. Using a clinical example, I speak of gender less as a determinate category than as something resembling a force field, that is, as a set of complex and shifting relations among multiple contrasts or differences. This multiplicity, in turn, generates some technical recommendations about gender and splitting. Recapturing split-off parts of the self therefore requires inhabiting its transitional spaces, including that in which gender is not a given but is in question. Moreover, I suggest, counterintuitively, that gender identity both seals the package of self and preserves all the self must lose and thus bridges undifferentiated archaic depths and selfhood. In transference and countertransference, both patient and analyst must also enter this paradoxical space, where they alternate between being gendered and being gender-free.

responseSet.title: Toward a Critical Relational Theory of Gender
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Toward a Critical Relational Theory of Gender

Virginia Goldner PHD

Virginia Goldner

102 East 22 Street, #10H
New York, New York 10010

Virginia Goldner  

This article analyzes and critiques the construct of gender as a psychoanalytic and cultural category. Without succumbing to a nonpsychoanalytic notion of androgyny, the argument developed here challenges the assumption that an internally consistent gender identity is possible or even desirable. Beginning with the idea that, from an analytic perspective, the construct of “identity” is problematic and implausible, because it denotes and privileges a unified psychic world, the author develops a deconstructionist critique of our dominant gender-identity paradigm. It is argued that gender coherence, consistency, conformity, and identity are culturally mandated normative ideals that psychoanalysis has absorbed uncritically. These ideals, moreover, are said to create a universal pathogenic situation, insofar as the attempt to conform to their dictates requires the activation of a false-self system.

An alternative, “decentered” gender paradigm is then proposed, which conceives of gender as a “necessary fiction” that is used for magical ends in the psyche, the family, and the culture. From this perspective, gender identity is seen as a problem as well as a solution, a defensive inhibition as well as an accomplishment. It is suggested that as a goal for analytic treatment, the ability to tolerate the ambiguity and instability of gender categories is more appropriate than the goal of “achieving” a single, pure, sex-appropriate view of oneself.

[[RunningHead]]

The Etiology of Boyhood Gender Identity Disorder: A Model for Integrating Temperament, Development, and Psychodynamics

Susan Coates PHD , Richard Friedman MD and Sabrina Wolfe PHD

Sabrina Wolfe

1090 Amsterdam avenue
New York, NY 10025

Sabrina Wolfe  

In this paper, we present a model for conceptualizing the etiology of boyhood gender identity disorder. We illustrate the model with a specific case of a three-year-old boy who developed a gender identity disorder in reaction to his mother's depression after she had an abortion. We describe how the temperament of the child, his reaction to a psychic trauma during a sensitive period of mental representational development, and multigenerational transmission of psychodynamics lead to a gender identity disorder. We view the cross-gender fantasy as a compromise formation for the management of separation anxiety and aggression, and we view its enactment in behavior, in part, as a defensive attempt to understand an unmetabolizable experience of aggression. This case offers an unusual window into understanding how interpersonal experience, particularly in the face of severe anxiety, becomes transformed into intrapsychic phenomena and how pathological beliefs both encode experience and construct psychic reality.

The Mind and the Body

Robert Stolorow PHD and George Atwood PHD

George Atwood

11726 San Vicente Blvd., Ste. 410

Dept. of Psychology

George Atwood  

This article explores the nature and determining conditions of the different relationships that may exist in experience between the mind and the body. Various boundaries and relationships forming between the subjectively experienced mind and body are disclosed through an examination of (1) the experience of affect, (2) the phenomenon of concretization, and (3) embodied selfhood and its derailments. It is shown that the analytic approach to such mind-body relationships must take into account the differing intersubjective contexts in which they originate.

responseSet.title: Response to Discussions
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response to Discussions

Stephen Mitchell PHD

Stephen Mitchell

Stephen Mitchell  

Clinical psychoanalysis is a process by which analysand and analyst live through, struggle to understand, and sort out the most difficult, ambiguous, conflict-ridden dimensions of living. Psychoanalytic theorizing, therefore, of necessity, is forced to address and grapple with the knottiest, most paradoxical, most elusive of human phenomena. “Self” can certainly lay claim to being the knottiest, most paradoxical, most elusive problem of them all, bridging, as both Grossman and Wolf have noted, the subjective and the objective, conscious and unconscious, past and present, experiential and systemic. The sequential development of our ideas about different facets of self and the cross-fertilization of those different ideas lead to syntheses that, in turn, keep changing in their encounter with the theory-crunching complexities of the psychoanalytic process.

I am very appreciative of the manner in which Grossman and Wolf have attempted to engage my thinking about self by exploring similarities and differences in relation to their own approaches. The greatest hazard of comparing different theoretical traditions is that one must renounce any hope of comprehensiveness and also necessarily involve oneself in reduction (not reductionism) and simplification (not in a simplistic fashion). So I am grateful and accept Grossman's broadening of the ways Freud and Freudian theory can be interpreted and understood and Wolf's clarification of aspects of Kohut's contributions and his own particular extensions of them. Together they have raised important questions for me and stimulated further thinking about self, some features of which I will sketch out in this discussion.

I would like to take as a provocative starting point Grossman's argument that I am not systematic enough about unconscious organization and its relation to conscious experiences of self. I think he is right.

Commentary on Philip Bromberg's “On Knowing One's Patient Inside Out”

Enid Balint Hon. FRCGP

Enid Balint

Raven House
Ramsbury
Marlborough
Wiltshire SN8 SPA
England

Enid Balint  

I am pleased to have been asked to take part in the dialogue arising out of Philip Bromberg's paper. I enjoy reading his papers because they are so clearly based on clinical experience and because his theories arise out of this. Both the analyst and the patient are experienced when one reads his papers, and the reader does not feel that he is intruding on the analysis but, rather, feels himself to be part of a dialogue that he can understand and with which he can identify. The words that the reader chooses in his comments have to be the right words, as do the words the analyst and patient use when they talk to one another.

I can comment on only a few of Bromberg's ideas and choose those that resonate with my own. Bromberg talks about unconscious communication as a transferential enactment of unsymbolized experience and about how, for certain patients in particular, past experience is not so much unconscious as frozen in time. I like the expression “frozen in time,” and I take it that Bromberg sees this as belonging to the unconscious part of the mind, not as a process in the unconscious but as a state of the mind. Such states may be able to be linked with states in the analysis in which associations are not possible and that have to be tolerated by the analyst, whose aim, indeed, is to bring to consciousness parts of the patient that had never been associated with his core, or the part he thinks of as himself. With regard to the term “self,” I would like to add here that for me the patient has a “self” but also an “I,” which can say “this is me” or “this is not me”; and so I see the self as owned by the patient rather than as the core of the patient, which I think of as “I and me.”

It is important to keep in mind that what is remembered by the patient but has never been thought—has never had words—must have been experienced by the patient in a different way from other events, the occurrence of which has perhaps been wiped out by the patient and so

[[RunningHead]]

Commentary on “Toward a Critical Relational Theory of Gender”

John Gagnon PHD

John Gagnon

Stony Brook, NY 11794

John Gagnon  

I wish to avoid the directly critical in what follows, since I find so much of what Virginia Goldner has written to be provocative, intelligent, and helpful. I differ in the desire to go further than she does in the deconstructive effort, to ask what the consequences are of opening the door unselectively to what is now treated as postmodernism in the form of deconstruction and relativism.1

There is a profound tension between social constructionism as an intellectual strategy and the view that the construction of gender and of gender differences is a universal principle of cultural life. I wonder if gender has always been a universal principle of cultural life or if the decision to construe the social relations of women and men as “gendered” is a late 20th-century conception. It seems to me that the gendering of social life as we now experience and name the relations between women and men is the result of a complex process of cultural and historical developments that have been associated with the emergence of Western, urban, industrial, consumer, and market oriented societies. We may be gendered, but were the social relations between women and men in other cultures (including historical cultures, such as 19th-century Persia or 16th-century Spain or 10th-century China) “gendered”? Of course such cultures appear to us to be “gendered,” but they surely did not appear to their members to be gendered. Much as men who had sex with men in

Dr. Gagnon is Professor of Sociology and Psychology at the State University of New York at Stony Brook.

1It is my view that the questions of the relation between meaning and action that provoked the postmodernist response are the very same questions that provoked the modernist response at the end of the 19th century. Modernism as a cultural movement was an extraordinarily productive evasion of the lack of an immanent connection between meaning and action; whether postmodernism will provide as interesting a cultural response remains an open question.

responseSet.title: Introduction
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Introduction

Philip Bromberg PHD

Philip Bromberg

Philip Bromberg  

When I was young,” Mark Twain wrote, “I could remember anything, whether it happened or not.” Inasmuch as we have supposedly lost this capacity in growing up, the ability to relish the humor in this line is a remarkable human achievement. As adults, we like to call it “imagination.” But how does it develop? Let us imagine a four-year-old boy in the back seat of a car who is returning home at night with his parents after visiting grandma. He turns from staring out of the car window and says to his half-dozing mother in the front seat, “Hey Mom, look! The moon is following me.” His mother, unruffled, replies without opening her eyes, “It really isn't, honey. It just looks that way.” An issue is not made of the fact that the others in the car know that the boy's experience is “only” an illusion. There is no need to force the distinction between illusion and reality, and there is no cause for alarm; the parents recognize that this distinction is not a matter that can be grasped by logic and that eventually the boy will somehow know it on his own. But just “knowing” reality is not what growing up is all about. We are all well aware from our work that “knowing” reality can be a disastrously grim experience for many people. If a child is routinely allowed, as in this story, comfortably to retain his subjective experience while engaging with his parents in his own way as they tell him about what is “really” happening, he stands a pretty good chance of growing into an adult who, like Mark Twain, always has a child along for the ride.

Language alone is insufficient to mediate developmental growth. New experience of self in relation to reality is organized not by ideas but by personal imagery. There must therefore be a communication with the outside that is at the affective level of the experience for linguistic content to become integrated pleasurably. The analytic situation is designed as a therapeutic relationship to bring about this integration.

[[RunningHead]]

Discussions

Ernest Wolf MD

Ernest Wolf

180 North Michigan Avenue
Chicago, IL 60601

Ernest Wolf  

Usually the birth of a science can be dated to the debut of a new idea, which, like a lusty infant who developed into an adult conquistador, now dominates a sphere of interest. Freud's original idea was that psychology, like physiology, was rooted in and determined by the total biological organism and that its mechanisms

Dr. Wolf is on the Faculty and is a Training and Supervising Analyst, Chicago Institute for Psychoanalysis.

160

could be studied scientifically in analogy to other biological sciences. One hundred years later it still is a truly revolutionary idea that the mind is not coextensive with consciousness but encompasses a vast realm of conscious, preconscious, and unconscious mental activity that is intimately bound up with the body. Indeed, it is so revolutionary that 100 years after Freud there are as yet few people to whom the unconscious mind is a reality as alive as the unconscious functioning of their bodies. Perhaps we should console ourselves by remembering that it took a couple of hundred years after Copernicus before the roundness of the earth replaced its former flatness for most people. Freud's idea has not conquered yet, but in the wake of Freud's death, and even before, there emerged a litter of related but sometimes incompatible propositions, each of which is claiming the imprimatur of being the exclusive bearer of Father Freud's heritage. To tame his babel of tongues Mitchell has set himself the daunting task of extracting a unifying vision of psychoanalysis by comparing and relating various perspectives on self in contemporary psychoanalytic thought. Alas, in the end he has to tell us that a neat and precise model of the self and its development have eluded him but that the array of ways of thinking about the generation of subjectivity enriches our participation in the infinite complexities of the psychoanalytic process.

Upon first reading Mitchell's “Contemporary Perspectives on Self,” I was overwhelmed by the richness and complexity of conceptions of the self and of the analytic process that Mitchell presents. As a psychoanalyst working in the Kohutian way of conceptualizing self and psychoanalytic self psychology, I was moved to reflect again on what I mean by self. To start, I make a sharp distinction between subjective experience and attempts at objective conceptualizations of that experience. Clearly, the experience and the conceptualization of that experience are not the same but are intimately related, like two sides of the same coin. Unfortunately the term self has been used to refer to both of these, with resulting confusion. The concept of the self as used in psychoanalytic self psychology refers to the psychological structure that results from the organization of a particular kind of experiences. These experiences have been designated “selfobject experiences” because they arise in connection with objects and evoke the experience of selfhood.

[[RunningHead]]

Discussions of “Contemporary Perspectives on Self: Toward an Integration”

William Grossman MD

William Grossman

45 Oak Avenue
Tenafly, NJ 07670

William Grossman  

Continuing his efforts to formulate a comprehensive “relational psychoanalysis,” Stephen Mitchell focuses on the concept of the “self” and reviews selected aspects of current views on the subject. He offers a challenging addition to recent discussions of object relations and the self. His paper deals directly with a few of the many theoretical, methodological, and clinical issues that have occupied the literature of the self in the last 20 years; however, the few aspects that Mitchell chooses for constructing his own views are essential ones. My discussion is necessarily limited to only a few points of agreement and disagreement that seem particularly important.

Early in his far-ranging and scholarly paper, Mitchell notes that there is a great deal of concern with the self in modern psychoanalysis and that this marks a difference from the concerns of classical psychoanalysis. This general concern, however, with what Schafer (1989) has called “the most popular figure in modern, innovative psychoanalytic accounts of human development and action” (p. 153) has, as Mitchell observes, produced little consensus as to what the term means. He rejects the idea that the term self does not mean anything. Instead, he suggests that self means many things. He adds that various alternative and allegedly competing characterizations of the self are, in fact, complementary. Therefore, he combines them to give a multifaceted view of self and of clinical approaches to problems of the self assembled from his presentations of various selected points of view.

Dr. Grossman is Training and Supervising Analyst, New York Psychoanalytic Institute; and Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Albert Einstein College of Medicine.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Therapeutic Relationship as a Paradoxical Experience

Arnold Modell MD

Arnold Modell

401 Woodward Street
Waban, MA 02168

Arnold Modell  

The therapeutic relationship exists within multiple levels of reality—including that of ordinary life and that of the therapeutic frame. This interplay between these two levels of reality gives rise to paradoxical experiences for both participants. Certain “principles” or “rules” of technique can be understood as a means of enabling the therapist to cope with what is usually referred to as “boundary” issues. It is essential that the analyst or therapist demonstrate capacity to shift playfully from one level of reality to another. The “rule” of abstinence and the asymmetry of desire that exists between the two participants are discussed. Gratification within the therapeutic frame is paradoxical in that gratification at one level of reality leads to privation at another level of reality. These paradoxical experiences for both patient and analyst are examined in relation to projective identification and to the analyst's countertransference.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Intimacy Between Men11

Emmanuel Kaftal PHD

Emmanuel Kaftal

315 Central Park West, #2E
New York, NY 10024

Emmanuel Kaftal  

The fantasy self-image of the hero is common to many men. When these men come to psychotherapy, they present a variety of symptoms that may be linked to this self-image. These include grandiosity, the need to control the therapist, empty depression, and a preoccupation with the imagery of death and battle. These patients tend to evoke in the therapist emotional responses that the patients subsequently deny. These characteristics are so pervasive among men that they may be endemic to manhood.

The heroic model of manhood is an attempt to strengthen and stabilize the gendered self-representation. Because fathers tend to be absent from the nurturing matrix, their sons have little early experience in an affective, preverbal relationship of mutual influence with another who is essentially like themselves yet outside their omnipotent control. Men, therefore, are raised with a pervasive experience of “otherness,” their infantile experience and affectivity forever trapped in the world of women. Although the yearning of men for their fathers is recognized in psychoanalytic theory, the wish for the nurturing father has been underemphasized. The underemphasis causes the analyst to miss, or misunderstand, important transferential constellations.

[[RunningHead]]

Commentary on Irwin Z. Hoffman's Discussion: “Toward a Social-Constructivist View of the Psychoanalytic Situation”

Jessica Benjamin PHD

Jessica Benjamin

228 West 22nd Street
New York, NY 10011

Jessica Benjamin  

I would like to respond to Hoffman's (1991) commentary on the papers by Modell, Greenberg, and Aron. The papers and the commentary are extremely rich, and I will address only a few key issues raised by Hoffman. In particular, I want to examine Hoffman's formulation of the new paradigm that defines contemporary psychoanalysis. His premise is that the great changes occurring in our field can be articulated in terms of one epistemiological principle, in this case from positivist to social-constructivist. To begin with, this premise raises a question as to the adequacy of any one epistemiological or metapsychological principle to grasp these changes. It seems to me that this kind of conceptualization misses the structure of many great changes in intellectual history. Such changes do not necessarily fit Kuhn's model of the structure of scientific revolutions, which involve the creation of a new paradigm. For example, the emergence of postmodernism embraces many intellectual and cultural movements; it can be characterized not by one single principle, but rather by a plurality of elements, most, but not all, of which may be present in any single work. The breadth of the changes in contemporary psychoanalysis, including the many disagreements among those who hold in common their rejection of “classical psychoanalysis,” more closely fits this pluralist configuration than it does the idea of a paradigm shift based on one underlying principle.

Does this mean that Hoffman's definition of the epistemological change we are undergoing is accurate but simply claims too much?

Dr. Benjamin is on the Faculty of the New York University Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis.

© 1991 The Analytic Press

[[RunningHead]]

Some Theoretical Comments on Personal Isolation

Thomas Ogden MD

Thomas Ogden

1721 Scott Street
San Francisco, CA 94115

Thomas Ogden  

The capacity for personal isolation is as critical to psychological health as is the capacity for object relatedness. In this paper, the notion of personal isolation is expanded to include a form of isolation in which the infant replaces the mother-as-environment with his own sensation matrix. This form of experience is not conceived of as a phase or stage of development prior to object relatedness; rather, it is viewed as an ongoing facet of all human experience that serves as a buffer against the continual strain of being alive in the world of human beings. The understanding of this primitive dimension of personal isolation provides a way of thinking about the differences between the elaboration of universal “autistic-contiguous” forms of experience and the development of pathological autism.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Countertransference and Reality

Jay Greenberg PHD

Jay Greenberg

275 Central Park West, 1BB
New York, NY 10024

Jay Greenberg  

The psychoanalytic situation provides many opportunities for people to observe their analysts closely. These observations are inevitably woven into the fabric of patients' transference experience. Because the observations can be uncomfortable for the analyst, there is a constant temptation to ignore or deny the plausibility of patients' perceptions. They can be, and often are, quickly reinterpreted as derivatives of sexual or aggressive urges. Psychoanalytic drive theory, with its emphasis on impulse rather than observation as the force behind transference experience, can encourage countertransferential disclaimers and lead to blind spots. Some technical suggestions are offered to avoid this tendency and are based on a relational understanding of the nature of transference.

[[RunningHead]]

Reply to Benjamin

Irwin Hoffman PHD

Irwin Hoffman

55 E. Washington Street
Suite 1217
Chicago, IL 60602

Irwin Hoffman  

If there are differences between Benjamin's views and my own, I think it is difficult to tell from her commentary precisely where the issues are joined. Part of the difficulty stems from the fact that, without acknowledging it, Benjamin makes many points that seem to be similar to, if not the same as, points that are in my discussion. For example, she develops the idea at some length of the importance of maintaining tension between opposites. At one point, she acknowledges one contribution to this view in my paper (where I discuss the interplay of mutuality and idealization [Hoffman, 1991, p. 92]), but the reader could hardly guess from her comments alone that a good deal of my essay, perhaps the heart of it, is devoted to framing the issues in dialectical terms. When she writes, “The perception of the other as an object is not eliminated, but hopefully it is counterbalanced or modified by the perception of the other as subject,” she could have cited my paper (e.g., Hoffman, 1991, p. 98) in support of her point. When she says, “Is [the skewing of understanding] not sometimes an induced result of the other's participation?” does she really mean to imply that I have a different view? If anything, I defended the usefulness of the term “countertransference,” despite Aron's objections to it, on the grounds that it connoted the salience in the analytic situation of the responsivity of the analyst to the patient's influence without, however, denying the simultaneous influence of the analyst on the patient. Similarly, I argued that the counterpoint to the emphasis in Aron's and Greenberg's papers on the importance of overcoming resistance to exploration of the patient's perceptions of the analyst is the importance of recognizing the healthy aspect of the resistance of both participants to the emergence of the analyst as a subject (Hoffman, 1991, pp. 90-92).

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Commentary on “Father and Daughter: Identification with Difference—A Contribution to Gender Heterodoxy

Irene Fast PHD

Irene Fast

580 Union Drive
Ann Arbor, MI 48109

Irene Fast  

Benjamin's topic is penis envy. Her focus is one aspect of the constellation of factors that Freud associated with it: the girl's wish, when she becomes aware of sex difference, to be a boy. Benjamin places this wish in the context of developments in gender identity and separation-individuation, which converge when girls are about 18-24 months of age. The girl's wish to be a boy, she suggests, is not responsive to feelings of genital inferiority as conceived in Freud's drive theory; conflicts about gender limits are a later development. The wish is a developmentally progressive identification with the father in the context of the girl's newly developing sense of personal will, intention, and agency.

Benjamin's own clinical observations and those of others, as well as patterns of relationship she finds in imaginative literature, suggest to her that this relationship takes a particular form. The girl wants to be recognized by her father as a subject of desire, a person who can want and act appropriately to fulfill her wants. In a relationship of identificatory love she wants to be accepted by him as his little boy.

Benjamin argues that the girl's turn to the father for recognition of herself as a subject of desire is rooted both in the family structure dominant in this society and in a phase-appropriate developmental conflict. In current family organization, she suggests, the mother is largely a source of goodness for the young child, and the father is a subject of desire. The girl, therefore, like the boy, finds in the father a mirror of her own sense of agency in a way not easily found in the mother.

Dr. Fast is Professor of Psychology at the University of Michigan.

In Quest of the Psychoanalytic Datum

Harold Boris

Harold Boris

Eight Warren Street
Lexington, MA 02173

Harold Boris  

Some defenses work so early and so massively that the experiences they lie athwart of or repress are difficult to find in the later transference. This paper deals with a method of reconstructing these by means of a systematic analysis of the analyst's own urges to make interpretations or other interventions. The conceptual foundation for this rationale, a differentiation of experiences when the individual is a member of the couple and when the pair, is provided.

An Interview with Thomas Ogden

This interview is the first in a series of interviews that will be a regular feature of Psychoanalytic Dialogues.

Thomas Ogden has been one of the most important and original contributors to psychoanalytic thought over the past decade. His first book, Projective Identification and Psychotherapeutic Technique (1982, Aronson) presented an innovative development and application of the key concept of projective identification as developed by Klein and Bion. Ogden explored the ways in which intrapsychic fantasies of the patient become actualized in the interpersonal field between patient and analyst, especially in work with schizophrenic patients. The Matrix of the Mind: Object Relations and the Psychoanalytic Dialogue (1986, Aronson) traced Ogden's explication and reworking of the basic concepts of the three major figures of the British school of object relations: Klein, Fairbairn, and Winnicott. His latest book, The Primitive Edge of Experience (1989, Aronson), presents Ogden's own synthesis and development of the central lines of theorizing derived from the British school and applied to areas such as the structure of experience, the Oedipus complex, the initial analytic meeting, and the nature of analytic knowledge.

Ogden's method has been consistent throughout. Deeply steeped in psychoanalytic tradition, he continually returns to, reworks, and enriches classical psychoanalytic concepts by applying to them a modern sensibility shaped by the contemporary intellectual climate and current clinical problems.
